message.content:
Review the plan for goal **G14** in **CONFIGURED MODE**: you are ONE OF SEVERAL reviewers — RETURN your verdict as a single fenced ```json block per the cq:plan-review rubric and write NOTHING to the reviews ledger (the orchestrator writes the single aggregated item).

Goal G14 (milestone M56) is a DEFECT-SEEDED fix for defect D65 (confirmed root cause: wanbond's default active-backup scheduler applies no egress pacing/AQM, so it lets the external Starlink last-mile buffer bloat to ~1s and drop ~13%, collapsing single-flow TCP to ~3.67 Mbps; compounding: no MSS clamp for forwarded TCP). Read the goal (fetch_item("goals", G14)), defect D65, and the emitted plan: work milestones M57 (pacing sched), M59 (config+wiring), M60 (docs+gate), M58 (MSS-clamp docs), and tasks T149–T159 (list them via list_milestone_items on each work milestone). Ground your judgment in the ACTUAL repo (internal/sched/, internal/config/config.go, internal/device/device.go, internal/bind/multipath.go, AGENTS.md gate).

Judge against the canonical rubric (fine-grained / correctly-sequenced / testable / grounded / complete): are the tasks small and correctly ordered by dependsOn; is each `acceptance` concrete and verifiable (a command/observable/invariant, not "works"); is the plan grounded in real symbols; does it completely address the confirmed root cause (both the pacing fix and the MSS-clamp compounding fault) with docs kept in sync and the real gate (nix develop -c just test + just lint across tags)? Flag any gaps as new_questions (user-only), criticism (autonomously fixable plan defects), or defects (out-of-scope faults). Emit the verdict JSON: {summary, verdict: "go-ahead"|"revise", new_questions:[], criticism:[], defects:[]}.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"item":{"id":"G14","milestoneId":"M56","status":"planning","fields":{"title":"Fix D65: shape egress on the default path + clamp forwarded-TCP MSS","description":"DEFECT-SEEDED goal (from confirmed root cause of D65 — skip clarification, proceed straight to planning).

CONFIRMED ROOT CAUSE: single-flow TCP collapses to ~3.67 Mbps because the loss + ~1s bufferbloat originate in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer, which wanbond's DEFAULT active-backup scheduler does nothing to shape (egress pacing/AQM is a weighted-only feature: internal/config/config.go:99-108, 218-223). The WAN carries ≥6.9 Mbps (UDP got 6.9 vs TCP 3.67 on the same path); the tunnel's encode CPU has 40-80x headroom (~2429 Mbps/core x86, ~160-300 Mbps/core Pi4) and there is no wanbond-internal queue (synchronous send: multipath.go:2027-2035) — so the unshaped sender overruns the Starlink buffer (build-to-1s-then-13%-drop = overflow signature) and TCP cwnd collapses. Compounding: no TCP MSS clamp is installed anywhere for FORWARDED traffic (daemon installs none: internal/device/device.go:52-55; deploy recipes omit it: docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, install.md, wanbond-fixes.md).

SUGGESTED FIX (verbatim from D65):
(1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally a CoDel-style AQM drop) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery (internal/sched/weighted.go token bucket + internal/config SizePacingFromBDP / BurstFrames), so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate and cannot self-inflict the ~1s Starlink bufferbloat. Expected: eliminate the standing queue, restore single-flow TCP toward the ≥6.9 Mbps the WAN carries. Design decision: wire pacing into active-backup directly vs expose a policy-independent pacing knob.
(2) SECONDARY — install a TCP MSS clamp on wanbond0 for forwarded traffic (mangle FORWARD -o wanbond0 TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu) and document it in the deploy recipes (install.md §9.2, wanbond-fixes.md). Design decision: daemon installs it for forwarded peers vs documented operator step.

VALIDATION: on-hardware three-way iperf3 (direct-WAN / tunnel / loopback) + loaded-RTT A/B on the Pi4/Starlink/o3 is deferred to verify/implement (the user waived pre-fix field measurement per Q56). NON-GOAL (low priority, NOT the D65 ceiling): send-path micro-optimizations (dst-reuse seam, GSO/sendmmsg batching, SIMD chacha20).","sourceRefs":["defects:D65"],"grounding":"Synthesized from opus+fable candidate planners (both grounded read-only in the repo). Key facts: the token-bucket pacer already lives inside WeightedScheduler (internal/sched/weighted.go: tokens[]/refillLocked/tryConsumeLocked/shedLocked, ClassControl-exempt per D22, PickPaced/PickNone sentinels already handled by internal/bind/multipath.go:1981-1989 as errPacerShedding — so NO Bind change needed). BDP sizing = config.SizePacingFromBDP (config.go:250); pacing is currently gated to PolicyWeighted (deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP config.go:955; applyDefaults/validate skip pacing knobs for active-backup). selectScheduler (device.go:786-810) builds ActiveBackup with only FailbackAfter (no pacer). Design decisions locked: (1) extract the pacer into a shared internal/sched component + make the pacing config policy-independent (reuse, not active-backup-internal duplication); (2) MSS clamp is a documented OPERATOR step, NOT daemon-installed (internal/device owns only the tunnel engine, no privileged shell-outs); (3) CoDel AQM deferred (token-bucket drop-at-head suffices for the overflow signature). Fail-fast: pacing_enabled under active-backup must NOT inherit the weighted synthetic ~10000fps default (an unbinding pace would reproduce D65). Gate = nix develop -c just build && just test && just lint (lint spans default+e2e+realhosts tags). On-hardware field validation deferred to a manual checklist per Q56.","milestones":["M57","M59","M60","M58"],"sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-131751-a939da48f71492819.md",".cq/logs/20260714-131751-a4e27ec8438296860.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-131751-a939da48f71492819.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-131751-a4e27ec8438296860.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:58:21.455Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:18:00.909Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}}

{"item":{"id":"D65","milestoneId":"M49","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"Tunnel single-flow throughput plateaus at ~3.67 Mbps with loss/reorder-limited TCP and ~1s bufferbloat under load","severity":"high","description":"Root-cause the tunnel's low single-flow throughput and its loss/bufferbloat under load.

**Observations (Pi 4 edge, Starlink active path ~40 ms RTT, o3 concentrator):**
- Single-stream **TCP** through the tunnel plateaus at **~3.67 Mbps**, with `cwnd` stuck at ~30 KB and **13 retransmits / 10 s** — i.e. loss/reordering-limited, not a clean rate cap.
- **UDP** offered at 8 Mbps delivers ~6.9 Mbps at **13% datagram loss** with the queue building to **~1 s loaded RTT** (idle ~40 ms).
- Plain WireGuard on aarch64 normally does far more than 3.67 Mbps, so the ceiling and the retransmit pattern warrant explanation.

**Investigate — isolate the ceiling's source and any fixable inefficiency:**
1. Separate the three candidate limiters with measurements: (a) direct iperf3 over the same WAN (no tunnel) = WAN ceiling; (b) tunnel over the WAN = end-to-end; (c) a loopback / netns tunnel (no WAN) = pure CPU/codec ceiling. Attribute the gap.
2. If CPU-bound: profile the send path — WG crypto vs reed-solomon FEC encode vs the bonding DATA-frame codec (`internal/bind/`) vs the userspace TUN read/write copy. Look for per-frame allocation, absence of send batching / GSO, single-goroutine serialization, or an XChaCha20/FEC hot loop.
3. If loss/reorder-bound: is the loss introduced by the tunnel (framing/FEC/scheduler) or by the WAN? Check MSS clamping on `wanbond0` (inner MTU 1400 → is TCP fragmenting/PMTU black-holing?), and whether active-backup ever briefly reorders at path selection.

Deliverable: a profile + the dominant cost, whether ~3.67 Mbps is the true CPU/WAN ceiling or an inefficiency, and a concrete fix candidate (batching, buffer reuse, MSS clamp) with the expected gain.","rootCause":"The single-flow throughput collapse is LOSS-INDUCED, not a CPU or raw-WAN rate cap, and the loss originates in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer that wanbond does nothing to shape.

(1) The WAN demonstrably carries ≥6.9 Mbps: UDP offered at 8 Mbps delivered 6.9 through the same tunnel/path while single-flow TCP got only 3.67, so TCP's shortfall is loss-induced cwnd collapse (cwnd stuck ~30KB over ~40ms RTT), not a rate cap [D65 measurements].
(2) The tunnel's own processing is not the ceiling: measured DATA-codec encode = 4610 ns/op ≈ 2429 Mbps/core on x86_64 (pprof: chacha20-dominated, generic non-SIMD path), ~160-300 Mbps/core extrapolated to Pi4 aarch64 — 40-80x above 3.67 Mbps; and 3.67 Mbps ≈ 300 pps is trivially within the syscall/lock budget [internal/frame inline benchmark].
(3) There is no wanbond-internal queue: Send writes each frame synchronously to the UDP socket [internal/bind/multipath.go:2027-2035], the pacer sheds at the head and is BDP-bounded [internal/config/config.go:218-223], and the resequencer is bounded in memory+latency [internal/reseq/reseq.go:90-96] — so the observed ~1s loaded RTT is the EXTERNAL Starlink buffer, and its build-to-~1s-then-~13%-drop is the classic buffer-overflow signature of an unshaped sender (random medium loss would not grow the queue).
(4) Under the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler wanbond applies NO egress pacing/AQM (pacing is a weighted-only feature) [internal/config/config.go:99-108], so it offers packets to the bloated last-mile unshaped and cannot prevent the overflow. This is the primary controllable root cause.
(5) Compounding, for FORWARDED (behind-tunnel) TCP no MSS clamp is installed anywhere — the daemon installs none [internal/device/device.go:52-55] and the deploy recipes omit it even for the client-LAN forwarding case [docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, docs/install.md, wanbond-fixes.md] — so forwarded segments can fragment/PMTUD-blackhole (Pi-originated TCP is already MSS-bounded by the TUN route MTU; only forwarded flows are affected).

Candidates H-B (scheduler reorder), H-C (CPU-bound encode), H-D (internal oversized queue), H-E (FEC overhead) were each ruled out with validated evidence (H5/H6/H7/H8 = wrong). The user reviewed this diagnosis (Q56) and authorized implementing the fixes directly, waiving prior field-measurement confirmation; exact quantified gain is deferred to on-hardware validation.","suggestedFix":"Two independent fixes (user authorized direct implementation via Q56):
(1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally a CoDel-style AQM drop) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery (internal/sched/weighted.go token bucket + internal/config SizePacingFromBDP / BurstFrames), so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate and cannot self-inflict the ~1s Starlink bufferbloat. Expected: eliminate the standing queue and restore single-flow TCP toward the ≥6.9 Mbps the WAN demonstrably carries. {"item":{"id":"D65","milestoneId":"M49","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"Tunnel single-flow throughput plateaus at ~3.67 Mbps with loss/reorder-limited TCP and ~1s bufferbloat under load","severity":"high","description":"Root-cause the tunnel's low single-flow throughput and its loss/bufferbloat under load.

**Observations (Pi 4 edge, Starlink active path ~40 ms RTT, o3 concentrator):**
- Single-stream **TCP** through the tunnel plateaus at **~3.67 Mbps**, with `cwnd` stuck at ~30 KB and **13 retransmits / 10 s** — i.e. loss/reordering-limited, not a clean rate cap.
- **UDP** offered at 8 Mbps delivers ~6.9 Mbps at **13% datagram loss** with the queue building to **~1 s loaded RTT** (idle ~40 ms).
- Plain WireGuard on aarch64 normally does far more than 3.67 Mbps, so the ceiling and the retransmit pattern warrant explanation.

**Investigate — isolate the ceiling's source and any fixable inefficiency:**
1. Separate the three candidate limiters with measurements: (a) direct iperf3 over the same WAN (no tunnel) = WAN ceiling; (b) tunnel over the WAN = end-to-end; (c) a loopback / netns tunnel (no WAN) = pure CPU/codec ceiling. Attribute the gap.
2. If CPU-bound: profile the send path — WG crypto vs reed-solomon FEC encode vs the bonding DATA-frame codec (`internal/bind/`) vs the userspace TUN read/write copy. Look for per-frame allocation, absence of send batching / GSO, single-goroutine serialization, or an XChaCha20/FEC hot loop.
3. If loss/reorder-bound: is the loss introduced by the tunnel (framing/FEC/scheduler) or by the WAN? Check MSS clamping on `wanbond0` (inner MTU 1400 → is TCP fragmenting/PMTU black-holing?), and whether active-backup ever briefly reorders at path selection.

Deliverable: a profile + the dominant cost, whether ~3.67 Mbps is the true CPU/WAN ceiling or an inefficiency, and a concrete fix candidate (batching, buffer reuse, MSS clamp) with the expected gain.","rootCause":"The single-flow throughput collapse is LOSS-INDUCED, not a CPU or raw-WAN rate cap, and the loss originates in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer that wanbond does nothing to shape.

(1) The WAN demonstrably carries ≥6.9 Mbps: UDP offered at 8 Mbps delivered 6.9 through the same tunnel/path while single-flow TCP got only 3.67, so TCP's shortfall is loss-induced cwnd collapse (cwnd stuck ~30KB over ~40ms RTT), not a rate cap [D65 measurements].
(2) The tunnel's own processing is not the ceiling: measured DATA-codec encode = 4610 ns/op ≈ 2429 Mbps/core on x86_64 (pprof: chacha20-dominated, generic non-SIMD path), ~160-300 Mbps/core extrapolated to Pi4 aarch64 — 40-80x above 3.67 Mbps; and 3.67 Mbps ≈ 300 pps is trivially within the syscall/lock budget [internal/frame inline benchmark].
(3) There is no wanbond-internal queue: Send writes each frame synchronously to the UDP socket [internal/bind/multipath.go:2027-2035], the pacer sheds at the head and is BDP-bounded [internal/config/config.go:218-223], and the resequencer is bounded in memory+latency [internal/reseq/reseq.go:90-96] — so the observed ~1s loaded RTT is the EXTERNAL Starlink buffer, and its build-to-~1s-then-~13%-drop is the classic buffer-overflow signature of an unshaped sender (random medium loss would not grow the queue).
(4) Under the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler wanbond applies NO egress pacing/AQM (pacing is a weighted-only feature) [internal/config/config.go:99-108], so it offers packets to the bloated last-mile unshaped and cannot prevent the overflow. This is the primary controllable root cause.
(5) Compounding, for FORWARDED (behind-tunnel) TCP no MSS clamp is installed anywhere — the daemon installs none [internal/device/device.go:52-55] and the deploy recipes omit it even for the client-LAN forwarding case [docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, docs/install.md, wanbond-fixes.md] — so forwarded segments can fragment/PMTUD-blackhole (Pi-originated TCP is already MSS-bounded by the TUN route MTU; only forwarded flows are affected).

Candidates H-B (scheduler reorder), H-C (CPU-bound encode), H-D (internal oversized queue), H-E (FEC overhead) were each ruled out with validated evidence (H5/H6/H7/H8 = wrong). {"items":{"goals":[{"id":"G14","milestoneId":"M56","status":"planning","fields":{"title":"Fix D65: shape egress on the default path + clamp forwarded-TCP MSS","description":"DEFECT-SEEDED goal (from confirmed root cause of D65 — skip clarification, proceed straight to planning).

CONFIRMED ROOT CAUSE: single-flow TCP collapses to ~3.67 Mbps because the loss + ~1s bufferbloat originate in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer, which wanbond's DEFAULT active-backup scheduler does nothing to shape (egress pacing/AQM is a weighted-only feature: internal/config/config.go:99-108, 218-223). The WAN carries ≥6.9 Mbps (UDP got 6.9 vs TCP 3.67 on the same path); the tunnel's encode CPU has 40-80x headroom (~2429 Mbps/core x86, ~160-300 Mbps/core Pi4) and there is no wanbond-internal queue (synchronous send: multipath.go:2027-2035) — so the unshaped sender overruns the Starlink buffer (build-to-1s-then-13%-drop = overflow signature) and TCP cwnd collapses. Compounding: no TCP MSS clamp is installed anywhere for FORWARDED traffic (daemon installs none: internal/device/device.go:52-55; deploy recipes omit it: docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, install.md, wanbond-fixes.md).

SUGGESTED FIX (verbatim from D65):
(1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally a CoDel-style AQM drop) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery (internal/sched/weighted.go token bucket + internal/config SizePacingFromBDP / BurstFrames), so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate and cannot self-inflict the ~1s Starlink bufferbloat. Expected: eliminate the standing queue, restore single-flow TCP toward the ≥6.9 Mbps the WAN carries. Design decision: wire pacing into active-backup directly vs expose a policy-independent pacing knob.
(2) SECONDARY — install a TCP MSS clamp on wanbond0 for forwarded traffic (mangle FORWARD -o wanbond0 TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu) and document it in the deploy recipes (install.md §9.2, wanbond-fixes.md). Design decision: daemon installs it for forwarded peers vs documented operator step.

VALIDATION: on-hardware three-way iperf3 (direct-WAN / tunnel / loopback) + loaded-RTT A/B on the Pi4/Starlink/o3 is deferred to verify/implement (the user waived pre-fix field measurement per Q56). NON-GOAL (low priority, NOT the D65 ceiling): send-path micro-optimizations (dst-reuse seam, GSO/sendmmsg batching, SIMD chacha20).","sourceRefs":["defects:D65"],"grounding":"Synthesized from opus+fable candidate planners (both grounded read-only in the repo). Key facts: the token-bucket pacer already lives inside WeightedScheduler (internal/sched/weighted.go: tokens[]/refillLocked/tryConsumeLocked/shedLocked, ClassControl-exempt per D22, PickPaced/PickNone sentinels already handled by internal/bind/multipath.go:1981-1989 as errPacerShedding — so NO Bind change needed). BDP sizing = config.SizePacingFromBDP (config.go:250); pacing is currently gated to PolicyWeighted (deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP config.go:955; applyDefaults/validate skip pacing knobs for active-backup). selectScheduler (device.go:786-810) builds ActiveBackup with only FailbackAfter (no pacer). Design decisions locked: (1) extract the pacer into a shared internal/sched component + make the pacing config policy-independent (reuse, not active-backup-internal duplication); (2) MSS clamp is a documented OPERATOR step, NOT daemon-installed (internal/device owns only the tunnel engine, no privileged shell-outs); (3) CoDel AQM deferred (token-bucket drop-at-head suffices for the overflow signature). Fail-fast: pacing_enabled under active-backup must NOT inherit the weighted synthetic ~10000fps default (an unbinding pace would reproduce D65). Gate = nix develop -c just build && just test && just lint (lint spans default+e2e+realhosts tags). On-hardware field validation deferred to a manual checklist per Q56.","milestones":["M57","M59","M60","M58"],"sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-131751-a939da48f71492819.md",".cq/logs/20260714-131751-a4e27ec8438296860.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-131751-a939da48f71492819.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-131751-a4e27ec8438296860.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:58:21.455Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:18:00.909Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}]}}

content.text: {"items":{"tasks":[{"id":"T152","milestoneId":"M59","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Generalize the [scheduler] pacing config to be policy-independent (with fail-fast)","description":"Make egress pacing a policy-independent config surface usable under the DEFAULT active-backup policy, not only weighted (D65 design decision: policy-independent knob). internal/config/config.go: (1) split the pacing knobs (PerPathCapacityFPS, PacingBurstFrames, PacingEnabled) from the weighted-only aggregation/weight knobs (engage/disengage/collapse/load_tau/weight_*). (2) Rename deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP → derivePacingFromBDP and change its gate from `Policy==PolicyWeighted && PacingEnabled` to `PacingEnabled` for BOTH policies, so per-path link_bandwidth+link_rtt BDP sizing (SizePacingFromBDP) fills PerPathCapacityFPS/PacingBurstFrames under active-backup too; keep the all-paths-or-none link_bandwidth rule, bottleneck sizing, and the raw-knobs-vs-link_bandwidth mutual-exclusion. (3) In applyDefaults, when policy is active-backup AND pacing_enabled, default the pacing knobs (today applyDefaults early-returns for non-weighted). (4) validate: enforce pacing_burst_frames>0 and per_path_capacity_fps>0 when pacing_enabled under active-backup, keeping weighted-only knobs inert/unvalidated under active-backup. FAIL-FAST (critical, from D65 mechanism): pacing_enabled under active-backup with NEITHER all-paths link_bandwidth+link_rtt NOR explicit per_path_capacity_fps+pacing_burst_frames is a LOAD ERROR — the weighted synthetic default (~10000 fps) must NOT silently apply under active-backup, because a nominally-enabled-but-UNBINDING pace would reproduce D65 while claiming to shape. Weighted-policy behavior stays byte-identical.","acceptance":"`nix develop -c just test` green with new config cases: (i) active-backup + pacing_enabled + explicit per_path_capacity_fps/pacing_burst_frames loads+validates; (ii) active-backup + pacing_enabled + link_bandwidth+link_rtt on ALL paths sizes the knobs from the bottleneck via SizePacingFromBDP; (iii) active-backup + pacing_enabled with NO bandwidth and NO explicit knobs FAILS at load with a named error (no silent 10000fps default); (iv) partial link_bandwidth and setting both raw knobs+link_bandwidth each fail fast; (v) active-backup WITHOUT pacing keeps every weighted knob zero/inert; (vi) all pre-existing weighted config tests pass unchanged.","suggestedModel":"frontier","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"],"dependsOn":["T150"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:16:26.891Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:24.616Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"},{"id":"T153","milestoneId":"M59","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Wire pacing config into selectScheduler's active-backup branch","description":"internal/device/device.go selectScheduler (the default/active-backup branch, ~device.go:803): pass the validated pacing configuration into sched.Config so a configured pace actually reaches ActiveBackup — set Pacing=cfg.Scheduler.PacingEnabled, PerPathCapacity=cfg.Scheduler.PerPathCapacityFPS, PacingBurst=cfg.Scheduler.PacingBurstFrames alongside the existing FailbackAfter. Leave the weighted branch unchanged. The existing health prober set suffices (active-backup needs only health). No other composition-root change.","acceptance":"`nix develop -c go build ./...` succeeds; a device/config-to-scheduler test asserts a config with policy=active-backup + pacing_enabled=true builds an ActiveBackup whose Pick sheds (PickPaced) under sustained overload, and pacing_enabled=false builds the byte-for-byte pre-change behavior. `nix develop -c just test` (incl. ./internal/device/...) green.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"],"dependsOn":["T150","T152"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:16:32.388Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:26.137Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"},{"id":"T154","milestoneId":"M59","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Config unit tests for active-backup pacing and BDP sizing","description":"Focused config-layer tests locking the new surface (complementary to those bundled with the generalization task): policy=active-backup + pacing round-trips through Load/normalize/validate; BDP sizing under active-backup produces the SAME bottleneck-governed CapacityFPS/BurstFrames as the weighted path for identical link_bandwidth/link_rtt inputs (parity table test); and an existing active-backup config with NO pacing continues to load+validate with all weighted knobs zero (regression guard that the P1 empty-config surface is unchanged).","acceptance":"`nix develop -c go test ./internal/config/ -run Pacing` passes with the active-backup cases; a table test asserts SizePacingFromBDP-derived values match between active-backup and weighted for equal inputs; the no-pacing active-backup regression case is green; `nix develop -c just test` green overall.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"],"dependsOn":["T152"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:16:37.344Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:28.187Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}]}}

Config key names must match across all files. Reference D65 as the motivating defect.","acceptance":"grep confirms README.md, docs/design.md, docs/install.md, docs/runbook.md, wanbond.example.toml each state pacing is available under active-backup with identical key names; the design.md decision block records the policy-independent-pacing choice; wanbond.example.toml still loads via the config tests; `nix develop -c just lint` (misspell/doc checks) passes on the changed files.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"],"dependsOn":["T152","T153"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:16:44.340Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:29.626Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"},{"id":"T156","milestoneId":"M60","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Add the D65 field-validation procedure to docs/manual-checklist.md","description":"The on-hardware validation was waived pre-fix (Q56) and deferred to the field pass — script it so it is executable verbatim: three-way iperf3 attribution (direct-WAN no-tunnel / through-tunnel / loopback-netns tunnel) plus a loaded-RTT A/B with pacing OFF vs ON, on the Pi4-edge/Starlink/o3 topology. Record the expected observations: single-flow TCP through the paced tunnel approaches the UDP-goodput ceiling (~6.9 Mbps measured pre-fix) instead of ~3.67 Mbps; loaded RTT no longer builds toward ~1s; retransmits drop from ~13/10s. content.text: {"items":{"tasks":[{"id":"T158","milestoneId":"M58","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Document the forwarded-TCP MSS clamp operator recipe","description":"Deliver the SECONDARY D65 fix as a documented OPERATOR step (design decision: the daemon owns only the tunnel engine and stays free of privileged shell-outs per the internal/device invariant, and the repo already treats ALL firewall/routing plumbing as operator-owned via the oneshot-unit pattern — so the clamp is an operator recipe, NOT a daemon-installed rule). Add to docs/install.md §9.2 (the full-tunnel / route-a-client-LAN forwarding recipe) and docs/runbook.md's firewall-persistence step the two rules verbatim from docs/p1-mtu.md: `iptables -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu` plus the ip6tables equivalent — on BOTH forwarding nodes (edge AND concentrator), scoped to FORWARDED traffic, with persistence guidance matching the recipe's existing pattern, a cross-reference to docs/p1-mtu.md's MSS-clamping section for the arithmetic, one line on why --clamp-mss-to-pmtu is preferred over --set-mss (tracks inner-MTU retuning), and an explicit statement that omitting it lets forwarded TCP emit segments that fragment/PMTU-blackhole (the D65 compounding fault). Keep the existing MSS=1361 accounting in p1-mtu.md consistent. Docs only.","acceptance":"grep confirms docs/install.md §9.2 and docs/runbook.md each contain BOTH clamp rules (iptables AND ip6tables) with -o wanbond0 and --clamp-mss-to-pmtu, a persistence note, a link to docs/p1-mtu.md, and name both edge and concentrator as clamp points; the recipe is an operator step (no daemon shell-out); `nix develop -c just lint` doc checks pass.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:07.335Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:07.335Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"},{"id":"T159","milestoneId":"M58","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Record the MSS-clamp gap closure in wanbond-fixes.md","description":"wanbond-fixes.md's C3 full-tunnel / route-a-client-LAN recipe (the deploy notes the D65 root cause cites as omitting the clamp) gains the TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu FORWARD rule as a REQUIRED step of the routed-client-LAN setup, marked as closing the D65 compounding fault, pointing at the now-updated docs/install.md §9.2 recipe rather than duplicating the rule syntax at length.","acceptance":"wanbond-fixes.md C3 (or an adjacent entry) names the TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu FORWARD rule as a required recipe step and references docs/install.md §9.2 and D65; `nix develop -c just lint` doc checks pass.","suggestedModel":"fast","ledgerRefs":["goals:G14","defects:D65"],"dependsOn":["T158"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:11.561Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T13:17:34.260Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}]}}

10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.

14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}

46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {

64		}

97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.

102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}

113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.

120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}

7	)
8	
9	// maxShards is the Reed-Solomon field limit: RS over GF(2^8) admits at most 256
10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.
11	const maxShards = 256
12	
13	// lenPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian length header prepended to each
14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}
45	
46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {
63			return fmt.Errorf("fec: Deadline must be > 0, got %s", c.Deadline)
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// GroupID identifies one FEC coding group. The encoder assigns group ids
69	// monotonically; the decoder buckets received shards by this id.
70	type GroupID uint32
71	
72	// Shard is the closed sum over the two kinds of frame an FEC group emits: a
73	// DataShard wrapping one opaque payload, and a ParityShard carrying redundancy.
74	// The unexported marker prevents other packages from introducing new kinds.
75	type Shard interface {
76		// GroupID returns the coding group this shard belongs to.
77		GroupID() GroupID
78		isShard()
79	}
80	
81	// DataShard is one opaque DATA payload admitted into a group, tagged with its
82	// coding coordinates so the decoder can place it. It carries no group cardinality
83	// (M), because a data frame is emitted immediately on admission — before the
84	// group's final size is known — to keep datapath latency low; the decoder learns
85	// M from any ParityShard of the group.
86	type DataShard struct {
87		Group   GroupID
88		Index   int    // position within the group, 0..M-1
89		Payload []byte // the opaque original bytes (never inspected)
90	}
91	
92	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
93	func (d DataShard) GroupID() GroupID { return d.Group }
94	func (DataShard) isShard()           {}
95	
96	// ParityShard is one Reed-Solomon parity frame for a group. It carries DataCount
97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.
100	type ParityShard struct {
101		Group     GroupID
102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}
106	
107	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
108	func (p ParityShard) GroupID() GroupID { return p.Group }
109	func (ParityShard) isShard()           {}
110	
111	// Recovered is a data payload the decoder reconstructed from parity — one that
112	// was NOT directly received. Directly-received data frames are never echoed back
113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.
114	type Recovered struct {
115		Group   GroupID
116		Index   int
117		Payload []byte
118	}
119	
120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}
```

45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;

115	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
116	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
117	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
118	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
119	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
120	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
121	const (
122		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
123		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
124		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
125		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
126		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
127		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
128		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
129		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
130		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
131		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
132		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
133		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
134		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
135		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
136		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
137		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
138		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
139		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
140		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
141		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
142		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
143		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
144		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
145		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
146		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
147		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
148		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
149		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
150		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
151		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
152		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
153		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
154		// alone rather than both diverging.
155		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
156	)
157	
158	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
159	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
160	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
161	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
162	type SchedulerConfig struct {
163		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
164		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
165	
166		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
167		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
168		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
169		// under the weighted policy.
170		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
171		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
172		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
173		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
174		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
175		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
176		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
177		// band.
178		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
179		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
180		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0. Parsed from CollapseDwellRaw in normalize.
181		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"-"`
182		// CollapseDwellRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of CollapseDwell, e.g.
183		// "2s" (D43). Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast, mirroring
184		// Path.LinkRTTRaw.
185		CollapseDwellRaw string `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
186		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0. Parsed
187		// from LoadTauRaw in normalize.
188		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"-"`
189		// LoadTauRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LoadTau, e.g. "200ms" (D43).
190		// Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast.
191		LoadTauRaw string `toml:"load_tau"`
192	
193		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
194		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
195		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
196		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
197		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
198		// when PacingEnabled.
199		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
200	
201		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
202		// Parsed from WeightRTTFloorRaw in normalize.
203		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"-"`
204		// WeightRTTFloorRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of WeightRTTFloor, e.g.
205		// "1ms" (D43). Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast.
206		WeightRTTFloorRaw string `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
207		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
208		// weighted).
209		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
210	}
211	
212	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
213	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
214	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
215	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
216	type BDPSizing struct {
217		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
218		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
219		CapacityFPS float64
220		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
221		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
222		// jitter without building a standing queue.
223		BurstFrames float64
224	}
225	
226	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
227	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
228	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
229	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
230	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
231	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
232	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
233	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
234	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
235	
236	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
237	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
238	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
239	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
240	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
241	//
242	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
243	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
244	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
245	//
246	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
247	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
248	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
249	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.

68	more header bytes → `InnerMTU6(1500) == 1381`. Deployments whose real path MTU is
69	below 1500 (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks — PPPoE is 1492, and CGNAT/telco tunnels are
70	often lower) must lower the path MTU passed to `InnerMTU`; when paths differ, size
71	the tunnel to the **smallest** path's inner MTU, since the single virtual endpoint

77	*behind* the tunnel (a LAN host routed through the edge, or the concentrator's
78	downstream) negotiate their MSS from their own link MTU and will happily send
79	1460-byte segments that no longer fit once wrapped. The fix is to clamp the TCP
80	MSS of forwarded SYNs to the tunnel's inner MTU minus the inner IP+TCP headers:
81	
82	```

For the default 1500-byte IPv4 path MTU:

```
inner MTU = 1500 − 28 (IP+UDP) − 39 (DATA frame) − 32 (WG) = 1401 bytes
```

`internal/bind.InnerMTU(1500) == 1401`, asserted by `TestInnerMTUFixture`, and
`internal/device` sizes the TUN to exactly this value. An IPv6 underlay costs 20
more header bytes → `InnerMTU6(1500) == 1381`. Deployments whose real path MTU is
below 1500 (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks — PPPoE is 1492, and CGNAT/telco tunnels are
often lower) must lower the path MTU passed to `InnerMTU`; when paths differ, size
the tunnel to the **smallest** path's inner MTU, since the single virtual endpoint
carries one MTU for the whole bond.

## MSS clamping (the operator action)

Setting the TUN MTU bounds what the *local* stack originates, but TCP endpoints
*behind* the tunnel (a LAN host routed through the edge, or the concentrator's
downstream) negotiate their MSS from their own link MTU and will happily send
1460-byte segments that no longer fit once wrapped. The fix is to clamp the TCP
MSS of forwarded SYNs to the tunnel's inner MTU minus the inner IP+TCP headers:

```
MSS = inner MTU − 40 (IPv4: 20 IP + 20 TCP) = 1401 − 40 = 1361 bytes
      inner MTU − 60 (IPv6: 40 IP + 20 TCP) = 1381 − 60 = 1321 bytes
```

Clamp on the forwarding node (edge and concentrator) with the standard
`TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu` target on the tunnel interface, or an explicit value:

```sh

iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \

          -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu

iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
          -j TCPMSS --set-mss 1361
```

`--clamp-mss-to-pmtu` is preferred: it derives the MSS from the tunnel interface
MTU, so it stays correct if the inner MTU is retuned (e.g. amnezia enabled, or a

1	# wanbond — production lessons (defects / improvements / docs)
2	
3	Context: real deploy of edge = Raspberry Pi 4 / Debian / **NetworkManager**, concentrator
4	= `o3` (aarch64, OCI, public 89.168.124.91 via NAT from private `enp0s6` 10.0.0.92). Two WAN
5	uplinks arrive at the Pi as VLAN sub-interfaces `eth0.231`(Starlink)/`eth0.232`(5G), each a
6	single-address interface, pinned per-WAN by `ip rule from <source_addr>`. MP client LAN =
7	`eth0.223` (192.168.223.0/24, Pi is .1). Goal reached: a bonded Starlink+5G tunnel to o3
8	(inner 10.77.0.0/24), then VLAN-223 client traffic routed through it and NAT'd out o3.
9	
10	Everything below was observed on real hardware/WANs. Unit + realhosts-P0 tiers are green;
11	these are all gaps that testbed (2 source IPs on ONE interface, both ends started fresh
12	together, never restarted independently) does not exercise.
13	
14	Severity: **S1** = tunnel silently non-functional / multi-minute outage; **S2** = wrong/cryptic
15	behavior with a workaround; **S3** = ergonomics/observability.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## A. DEFECTS (wrong behavior — need a code fix)
20	
21	### D1 (S1) — `allowed_ips = 0.0.0.0/0` wedges the handshake; tunnel never establishes
22	Bisected and confirmed. With the concentrator peer's `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` on the
23	edge, the WG handshake never completes — even fresh-restarting **both** ends and waiting
24	minutes (edge `tx≈0`, o3 `rx` floods to 2.3 MB with `tx` 9 KB, i.e. o3 receives but never
25	answers; no handshake ever logged). Reverting the same peer to a concrete prefix
26	(`10.77.0.1/32`) establishes in ~25 s, deterministically. Workaround that also works: the
27	**split default** `["0.0.0.0/1","128.0.0.0/1"]`. The virtual-endpoint design (engine never
28	holds the real 89.168.124.91) rules out an endpoint routing-loop, so the cause is a
29	`0.0.0.0/0`-specific path (amneziawg-go allowed-ip trie or a wanbond special-case). **This
30	silently breaks the single most common full-tunnel config.**
31	
32	### D2 (S1) — One-sided restart → multi-minute outage (no prompt re-handshake)
33	Confirmed repeatedly. When **either** end restarts, the peer still holding the old session
34	does not promptly re-handshake; the tunnel stays down for minutes until a rekey timer fires.
35	Restart edge → o3 stale → down for minutes; restart o3 → edge stale → down for minutes; only
36	restarting **both** ~together (both re-initiate from scratch) converges in ~25 s. For a
37	WAN-failover product this is severe — any reboot of the Pi or the concentrator is a
38	multi-minute client outage. Likely fix: force a fresh handshake on startup **and** on the
39	first path-up, and/or have the receiver treat a new valid init from a known static key as
40	immediately superseding the current session.
41	
42	### D3 (S2) — Startup handshake fires before path liveness, then isn't aggressively retried
43	On every start the edge logs one `Failed to send handshake initiation: bind: no healthy
44	path with a known remote endpoint` (paths not up yet), paths transition up ~0.6 s later —
45	and then nothing. The first init is wasted and re-initiation is not visibly driven off the
46	path-up edge, compounding D2. WG should (re)initiate the moment the first path becomes live.
47	
48	### D4 (S1) — Auto device-bind silently defeats source-policy routing
49	`selectDeviceBinds` picks `SO_BINDTODEVICE` (wildcard source) for a one-address/one-path
50	interface — exactly a VLAN-per-WAN edge. A wildcard-source socket never matches `ip rule
51	from <source_addr>`, so the lookup falls to `main` (no route to the concentrator via the
52	VLAN) → ENETUNREACH → silent path-down, zero packets on the wire, while `ping -I
53	<source_ip>` proves the WAN works. Fixed here with `ip rule ... oif <dev> table N`.
54	
55	### D5 (S2) — `wanbond0` left DOWN + unaddressed; NetworkManager then flushes the address
56	The daemon creates the tun but never brings it up or addresses it. On an NM host the tun is
57	auto-managed: NM **flushes** the operator's address on link-up and the interface stays
58	**DOWN** across daemon restarts. Writing to it yields the cryptic `TUN write: input/output
59	error`. Fixed with NM `unmanaged-devices` + an addressing oneshot.
60	
61	### D6 (S3) — `CAP_NET_RAW` (code) vs `CAP_NET_ADMIN` (shipped unit) mismatch
62	`pathsock` says `SO_BINDTODEVICE` needs `CAP_NET_RAW`; the shipped systemd units grant only
63	`CAP_NET_ADMIN`. Per the comment device-bind should always fall back, yet it bound
64	successfully on o3 — so the requirement is kernel-dependent and the comment/unit disagree.
65	Reconcile (grant `CAP_NET_RAW`, or fix the comment).
66	
67	---
68	
69	## B. IMPROVEMENTS (behavior/observability/ergonomics — not strictly a bug)
70	
71	### I1 — Daemon should bring the `wanbond0` link UP
72	It already creates the device; leaving it DOWN is a silent-dead-tunnel footgun (see D5).
73	Link-up is low-risk; addressing can stay operator-owned.
74	
75	### I2 — Expose a session/handshake state signal
76	The single biggest debugging obstacle: `path_up` goes to 1 long before the WG session
77	exists, and the only other signals are raw byte counters. Add `wanbond_session_established`
78	(and/or last-handshake-age) metric + a `handshake complete`/`session up` log line, so
79	"still converging" is distinguishable from "wedged" (D1/D2/D3 all presented identically).
80	
81	### I3 — Actionable error instead of raw `input/output error`
82	On TUN write, check interface state / MTU and emit e.g. `wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring
83	it up (install.md §4)` rather than EIO (D5).
84	
85	### I4 — Downgrade startup `no healthy path` ERROR spam
86	Logged at ERROR during the normal ~1 s liveness warmup; reads as a hard failure. DEBUG/INFO
87	during warmup, or a single `waiting for path liveness` line (D3).
88	
89	### I5 — Config toggle to force source-IP binding (opt out of device-bind)
90	For source-policy-routing topologies the roam-survival benefit of device-bind is moot and it
91	actively breaks routing (D4). A per-path/global `bind = "source"` switch avoids the whole
92	oif-rule workaround.
93	
94	### I6 — Native full-tunnel support
95	Once D1 is fixed, accept `0.0.0.0/0`; better, let the daemon apply the split internally, or
96	provide a `mode = "default-route"` that wires client routing (see docs C3) so operators
97	don't hand-assemble policy-route + SNAT + concentrator NAT.
98	
99	### I7 — Interface/route persistence across daemon restarts
100	Recreating `wanbond0` on every restart drops every address/route/rule referencing it, forcing
101	an external oneshot to rebuild them each time (addressing, `table 223` default, SNAT). Keep
102	the tun persistent across restarts, or ship the oneshot (see C4).
103	
104	### I8 — Verify standby-path liveness is bidirectional
105	Observed `path_up{5g}=1` with `tx{5g}=0` (edge sends nothing on the standby). Confirm a
106	"up" standby can actually transmit before failover selects it, else failover could pick a
107	path that only proved receive.
108	
109	---
110	
111	## C. DOCUMENTATION UPDATES
112	
113	### C1 — NetworkManager section (install.md §4)
114	Docs are networkd-only; most edge boxes (RPi OS/Debian/Ubuntu desktop) run NM. Add:
115	`unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0` in `/etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/`, or NM strips

117
